abstract: Spatial transcriptomics technologies provide critical gene expression profiles within their original spatial context, necessitating computational tools capable of effectively handling spatial clustering, multisample integration, and cell-type deconvolution. We present GraphST, a graph self-supervised contrastive learning framework designed to maximize the information extracted from spatial transcriptomics data. By integrating graph neural networks with self-supervised learning, GraphST generates highly informative and discriminative spot representations by minimizing the embedding distances between spatially adjacent neighbors. Through extensive benchmarking across diverse tissue types and diverse technology platforms, we demonstrate that GraphST consistently outperforms existing methods. Locally, GraphST achieved a 10% improvement in clustering accuracy while successfully delineating fine-grained anatomical structures in brain and embryo tissues. Furthermore, GraphST serves as a versatile tool for multisample analysis, uniquely supporting both vertical and horizontal integration while effectively correcting for batch effects. Finally, GraphST exhibits superior performance in cell-type deconvolution, accurately capturing complex spatial niches such as germinal centers in lymph nodes and the localization of exhausted tumor-infiltrating T cells in breast cancer.
